message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Evaluate antithrombotic (AT) use in individuals with atrial fibrillation (AF) and high stroke risk (CHA
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    DS
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    -VASc score>=2) and investigate whether pre-existing AT use may improve COVID-19 outcomes.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Individuals with AF and a CHA
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    DS
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    -VASc score>=2 on January 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    2020 were identified using pseudonymised, linked electronic health records for 56 million people in England and followed-up until May 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    2021. Factors associated with pre-existing AT use were analysed using logistic regression. Differences in COVID-19 related hospitalisation and death were analysed using logistic and Cox regression for individuals exposed to pre-existing AT use vs no AT use, anticoagulants (AC) vs antiplatelets (AP) and direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) vs warfarin.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    From 972,971 individuals with AF and a CHA
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    DS
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    -VASc score>=2, 88.0% (n=856,336) had pre-existing AT use, 3.8% (n=37,418) had a COVID-19 related hospitalisation and 2.2% (n=21,116) died. Factors associated with no AT use included comorbidities that may contraindicate AT use (liver disease and history of falls) and demographics (socioeconomic status and ethnicity). Pre-existing AT use was associated with lower odds of death (OR=0.92
                    <jats:italic>[0</jats:italic>
                    .
                    <jats:italic>87-0</jats:italic>
                    .
                    <jats:italic>96 at 95% CI]</jats:italic>
                    ), but higher odds of hospitalisation OR=1.20
                    <jats:italic>[1</jats:italic>
                    .
                    <jats:italic>15-1</jats:italic>
                    .
                    <jats:italic>26 at 95% CI]</jats:italic>
                    ). The same pattern was observed for AC vs AP (death (OR=0.93 [0.87-0.98]), hospitalisation (OR=1.17 [1.11-1.24])) but not for DOACs vs warfarin (death (OR=1.00 [0.95-1.05]), hospitalisation (OR=0.86 [0.82-0.89]).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Pre-existing AT use may offer marginal protection against COVID-19 death, with AC offering more protection than AP. Although this association may not be causal, it provides further incentive to improve AT coverage for eligible individuals with AF.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>KEY QUESTIONS</jats:title>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>What is already known about this subject?</jats:title>
                    <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>
                          Anticoagulants (AC), a sub-class of antithrombotics (AT), reduce the risk of stroke and are recommended for individuals with atrial fibrillation (AF) and at high risk of stroke (CHA
                          <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                          DS
                          <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                          -VASc score>=2, National Institute for Health and Care Excellence threshold). However, previous evaluations suggest that up to one third of these individuals may not be taking AC. Over estimation of bleeding and fall risk in elderly patients have been identified as potential factors in this under medicating.
                        </jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>In response to the COVID-19 pandemic, several observational studies have observed correlations between pre-existing AT use, particularly anticoagulants (AC), and lower risk of severe COVID-19 outcomes such as hospitalisation and death. However, these correlations are inconsistent across studies and have not compared all major sub-types of AT in one study.</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                    </jats:list>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>What does this study add?</jats:title>
                    <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>This study uses datasets covering primary care, secondary care, pharmacy dispensing, death registrations, multiple COVID-19 diagnoses routes and vaccination records for 56 million people in England and is the largest scale evaluation of AT use to date. This provides the statistical power to robustly analyse targeted sub-types of AT and control for a wide range of potential confounders. All code developed for the study is opensource and an updated nationwide evaluation can be rapidly created for future time points.</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>
                          In 972,971 individuals with AF and a CHA
                          <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                          DS
                          <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                          -VASc score>=2, we observed 88.0% (n=856,336) with pre-existing AT use which was associated with marginal protection against COVID-19 death (OR=0.92
                          <jats:italic>[0</jats:italic>
                          .
                          <jats:italic>87-0</jats:italic>
                          .
                          <jats:italic>96 at 95% CI]</jats:italic>
                          ).
                        </jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                    </jats:list>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>How might this impact on clinical practice?</jats:title>
                    <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>These findings can help shape global AT medication policy and provide population-scale, observational analysis results alongside gold-standard randomised control trials to help assess whether a potential beneficial effect of pre-existing AT use on COVID-19 death alters risk to benefit assessments in AT prescribing decisions.</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                    </jats:list>
                  </jats:sec>
                </jats:sec>
title: Evaluation of antithrombotic use and COVID-19 outcomes in a nationwide atrial fibrillation cohort
